title: Focus on state building: Song China
description: Read passages and answer questions about Song China.
topic_path: Focus on state building: Song China

assessment_items.question_content:
Before answering the question, read the following excerpt.

_Note: The Song Chinese considered themselves ethnically Han. This comes from the cultural and linguistic foundation established by the [Han Dynasty](https://www.khanacademy.org/humanities/world-history/ancient-medieval/zhou-qin-han-china/v/zhou-qin-and-han-dynasties). Most Chinese today still consider themselves ethnically Han._ 


> "When the Song dynasty came into power in 960, it had to accept a geopolitical system in which no state dominated all its neighbors. The Song continually had to negotiate power with the alien states on its borders. (...) 

> This fear of alien regimes, particularly of the Liao and Xi Xia to the north, impeded the ability of the Han Chinese to recognize the regional achievements of these peoples. The “barbarians” were viewed as uncivilized intruders who disturbed the proper course of history, which meant Chinese history exclusively--the only history considered worthy of being investigated and recorded."

From Dieter Kuhn, _The Age of Confucian Rule: The Song Transformation of China_ (Cambridge: The Belknap Press of Harvard University Press, 2009), 20. 


**Which choice best describes the states surrounding the Song dynasty?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Not all of the states were part of the Liao empires; they also included the Xi Xia.

choices.content: They were separate states but presented a united front against the Song
They were distinct political and cultural powers that the Song had to deal with individually
They represented a variety of Liao empires that the Song had to negotiate with
They were all violent and were constantly trying to take power away from the Song

assessment_items.question_content:
Before answering the question, read the following excerpt.

_Note: The Song Chinese considered themselves ethnically Han. This comes from the cultural and linguistic foundation established by the [Han Dynasty](https://www.khanacademy.org/humanities/world-history/ancient-medieval/zhou-qin-han-china/v/zhou-qin-and-han-dynasties). Most Chinese today still consider themselves ethnically Han._ 

> "When the Song dynasty came into power in 960, it had to accept a geopolitical system in which no state dominated all its neighbors. The Song continually had to negotiate power with the alien states on its borders. (...) 

> This fear of alien regimes, particularly of the Liao and Xi Xia to the north, impeded the ability of the Han Chinese to recognize the regional achievements of these peoples. The “barbarians” were viewed as uncivilized intruders who disturbed the proper course of history, which meant Chinese history exclusively--the only history considered worthy of being investigated and recorded."

From Dieter Kuhn, _The Age of Confucian Rule: The Song Transformation of China_ (Cambridge: The Belknap Press of Harvard University Press, 2009), 20. 


**The Song Chinese people’s description of outside groups as “barbarians” and their unwillingness to recognize any history outside of Chinese history indicates that**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: If the Song Chinese weren’t willing to recognize history outside of Chinese history, it’s unlikely that they had a deep understanding of what the people in the outside groups were like.

choices.content: the Song envied the northern culture and elites prohibited their study to prevent people from moving away
the Song Chinese had a deep understanding of what the people in these outside groups were like
the Song Chinese had a strong sense of cultural unity or identity
the barbarian groups had committed atrocities against the Chinese and the Chinese refused to recognize their history

assessment_items.question_content:
Before answering the question, read the following excerpt.

> "In Song times, under the influence of both Buddhism and Daoism, Confucian thinkers began to reorganize ancient Chinese thought and create the basis of a philosophical system known as the Learning of the Way. Far more than a single philosophical school, this movement -- often labeled neo-Confucianism in the West -- aimed primarily to establish a social and political order. It defined and reasserted a system of Chinese values . . . which served as the pivot for Song culture in both the public and private spheres.

> The thinkers responsible for this “renaissance” attempted to reestablish the superiority of Confucian philosophy over its Buddhist and Daoist rivals by first clearing out the errors that had infiltrated Confucian learning over the centuries. But during this practical process of rejuvenation, they incorporated aspects of Buddhist and Daoist doctrine and practice that humanized the Confucian program. Throughout the Song dynasty, Confucianism, Daoism, and Buddhism were known as the “three doctrines,” and each played a role at every level of society."

From Dieter Kuhn, _The Age of Confucian Rule: The Song Transformation of China_ (Cambridge: The Belknap Press of Harvard University Press, 2009), 99.


**Which tradition(s) does Kuhn say influenced Song policy, and how?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Kuhn says there were “three doctrines” that influenced Song policy.

choices.content: the teachings of Confucianism fractured the Song dynasty into distinct parts
a blend of Confucian, Buddhist, and Daoist traditions united the Song Chinese
people united around studying the teachings of Daoism and Buddhism instead of Confucianism
people chose to follow only one of three doctrines: either Confucianism, Daoism, or Buddhism, causing a cultural breakdown

assessment_items.question_content:
Before answering the question, read the following excerpt.

> "In Song times, under the influence of both Buddhism and Daoism, Confucian thinkers began to reorganize ancient Chinese thought and create the basis of a philosophical system known as the Learning of the Way. Far more than a single philosophical school, this movement -- often labeled neo-Confucianism in the West -- aimed primarily to establish a social and political order. It defined and reasserted a system of Chinese values . . . which served as the pivot for Song culture in both the public and private spheres.

> The thinkers responsible for this “renaissance” attempted to reestablish the superiority of Confucian philosophy over its Buddhist and Daoist rivals by first clearing out the errors that had infiltrated Confucian learning over the centuries. But during this practical process of rejuvenation, they incorporated aspects of Buddhist and Daoist doctrine and practice that humanized the Confucian program. Throughout the Song dynasty, Confucianism, Daoism, and Buddhism were known as the “three doctrines,” and each played a role at every level of society."

From Dieter Kuhn, _The Age of Confucian Rule: The Song Transformation of China_ (Cambridge: The Belknap Press of Harvard University Press, 2009), 99.

**Which of the following claims about the relationship between philosophical, cultural, and political systems does Kuhn’s argument support?** 

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Kuhn says the three doctrines had a profound impact on a couple different elements of Song China.

choices.content: it can have a profound impact on an empire’s cultural and political unity
it can have a profound impact on an empire’s cultural, but not political, unity
it can have a profound impact on an empire’s political, but not cultural, unity
it cannot impact an empire’s political or cultural unity in a meaningful way

assessment_items.question_content:
The following question is based on the two passages referenced in this section.

> "When the Song dynasty came into power in 960, it had to accept a geopolitical system in which no state dominated all its neighbors. The Song continually had to negotiate power with the alien states on its borders. (...) 

> This fear of alien regimes, particularly of the Liao and Xi Xia to the north, impeded the ability of the Han Chinese to recognize the regional achievements of these peoples. The “barbarians” were viewed as uncivilized intruders who disturbed the proper course of history, which meant Chinese history exclusively--the only history considered worthy of being investigated and recorded."

From Dieter Kuhn, _The Age of Confucian Rule: The Song Transformation of China_ (Cambridge: The Belknap Press of Harvard University Press, 2009), 20. 

> "In Song times, under the influence of both Buddhism and Daoism, Confucian thinkers began to reorganize ancient Chinese thought and create the basis of a philosophical system known as the Learning of the Way. Far more than a single philosophical school, this movement -- often labeled neo-Confucianism in the West -- aimed primarily to establish a social and political order. It defined and reasserted a system of Chinese values . . . which served as the pivot for Song culture in both the public and private spheres.

> The thinkers responsible for this “renaissance” attempted to reestablish the superiority of Confucian philosophy over its Buddhist and Daoist rivals by first clearing out the errors that had infiltrated Confucian learning over the centuries. But during this practical process of rejuvenation, they incorporated aspects of Buddhist and Daoist doctrine and practice that humanized the Confucian program. Throughout the Song dynasty, Confucianism, Daoism, and Buddhism were known as the “three doctrines,” and each played a role at every level of society."

From Dieter Kuhn, _The Age of Confucian Rule: The Song Transformation of China_ (Cambridge: The Belknap Press of Harvard University Press, 2009), 99.

**Which of the following best describes how foreign ideas were usually dealt with in Song China?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Although outside groups were often feared or looked down on, the Song Chinese used Buddhism, a foreign religion, as a way to humanize the strict Confucian doctrine and create a unifying philosophy.

choices.content: Foreign ideas were often feared by many, but were also incorporated into Song policy and culture when convenient
Foreign ideas were openly embraced by most Song Chinese
Foreign ideas were often feared by many, so they barely had any influence on Song policy or culture
Foreign ideas were frequently discussed in Song China, but rarely were they actually incorporated into Song policy or culture
